g.data-narration: Welcome to Latin-Square Design, a powerful experimental design that controls two nuisance variables simultaneously.
A Latin-square design arranges treatments in a square grid where each treatment appears exactly once in each row and column.
g.text: A special experimental design that controls for TWO sources of extraneous variation
(nuisance variables) while testing the effect of a single treatment factor.
p × p grid with p treatments
Each treatment appears once per row and column

g.data-narration: Use Latin-Square Design when you have exactly three factors at equal levels and want to control two blocking variables.
g.text: Two nuisance factors (blocking variables) affect the response
Number of treatment levels equals number of blocking factor levels
Example: Testing 4 fertilizers, controlling for field location (rows) and time (columns)

g.data-narration: Here's a 4×4 Latin Square example. Notice how treatment A appears exactly once in each row and column.
g.text: Each row has A,B,C,D exactly once
Each column has A,B,C,D exactly once
This balance removes row and column effects!

g.data-narration: A 3×3 Latin Square follows the same principle. The cyclic pattern A→B→C, B→C→A, C→A→B creates the balance.
g.text: Pattern: Each treatment shifts one position to the right in each successive row.

g.data-narration: The statistical model decomposes each observation into overall mean, row effect, column effect, treatment effect, and error.
g.text: xij(k) = μ + αi + βj + τk + eij(k)

g.data-narration: We test null hypotheses that all effects are zero. For treatments, at least one treatment effect differs from zero if rejected.
g.text: H0: τ1 = τ2 = ... = τp = 0 (no treatment effect)

g.data-narration: Total variation splits into four components: rows, columns, treatments, and error. This partitioning is fundamental to ANOVA.
The correction factor simplifies calculations. CF equals grand total squared divided by total observations.
g.text: CF = G2 / N where G = Grand Total, N = p2

g.data-narration: Degrees of freedom follow specific formulas. Remember: error df equals (p-1)(p-2), a key exam formula.
Mean squares divide SS by respective degrees of freedom. F-statistic for treatments equals treatment MS divided by error MS.
g.text: MSR = SSR/(p-1) MSC = SSC/(p-1) MSTr = SSTr/(p-1) MSE = SSE/[(p-1)(p-2)]

g.data-narration: The ANOVA table summarizes all calculations: sources, sum of squares, degrees of freedom, mean squares, and F-values.
Let's work through a complete 4×4 example. Here are the observed values for each treatment in the Latin square layout.
We compute totals for each treatment: A=39, B=72, C=91, D=61. Then calculate correction factor and sum of squares.
